did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a Probabilistic Neural Network (PNN) classifier as part of an adaptable pattern recognition system to discriminate between melanocytic nevi and malignant melanomas using plain photography images. The system employed feature extraction (texture, structure, shape, color) and exhaustive search feature selection, evaluated via leave-one-out and external cross-validation methods.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the design and validation of an adaptable pattern recognition system (using Probabilistic Neural Networks) to discriminate melanocytic nevi from malignant melanomas. It details specific materials and methods (image databases from Edinburgh, Multicenter, Groningen), feature extraction, statistical analysis, and reports new quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity) from leave-one-out and external cross-validation experiments.

title: Adaptable pattern recognition system for discriminating Melanocytic Nevi from Malignant Melanomas using plain photography images from different image databases
brief_summary: This study aimed to develop an adaptable pattern recognition system to discriminate between melanocytic nevi and malignant melanomas using plain photography images from diverse databases. The researchers utilized digital images from the Edinburgh University Hospital (Dermoﬁt), Multicenter, and Groningen University datasets, extracting fourteen features evaluating texture, structure, shape, and color. A Probabilistic Neural Network classifier with exhaustive search feature selection was employed, validated via leave-one-out and external cross-validation methods. Statistical analysis indicated that melanomas exhibited lower intensity, less homogeneity, and more irregular shapes compared to moles. The system achieved high accuracy on the training Dermoﬁt dataset (94.1%) and maintained good performance on external Multicenter (92.0%) and Groningen (76.2%) datasets. The results demonstrate that the system can be fine-tuned to classify lesions from different databases despite variations in image capturing equipment and protocols.

lesion_types_studied.details: Benign lesions used as the comparator class to malignant melanomas. Counts: 330 in Dermoﬁt dataset, 63 in Multicenter dataset, 100 in Groningen dataset.
Malignant lesions used as the target class to discriminate from moles. Counts: 70 in Dermoﬁt dataset, 25 in Multicenter dataset, 70 in Groningen dataset.

all_task_types.target: Discrimination between melanocytic nevi (moles) and malignant melanomas
all_task_types.details: Binary classification task using a Probabilistic Neural Network (PNN) classifier to discriminate between two discrete diagnostic categories: benign melanocytic nevi and malignant melanomas. The system was trained on the Dermoﬁt database and validated on external databases (Multicenter and Groningen) using leave-one-out and external cross-validation methods. Performance metrics reported include overall accuracy, sensitivity, and specificity.

datasets_used.name: Dermoﬁt
datasets_used.description: Digital plain photography images of verified mole and melanoma lesions downloaded from Edinburgh University Hospital, UK, under signed agreement.

use.training: Used to design the PR-system (Probabilistic Neural Network classifier) and for internal validation via external cross-validation (ECV).
use.validation: Used for internal validation via external cross-validation (ECV) to score performance on the same dataset.
use.other: Used as the source database for designing the adaptable pattern recognition system.

datasets_used.name: Multicenter
datasets_used.description: Digital plain photography images of verified mole and melanoma lesions from 5 different centers, publicly available.

use.testing: Used for external cross-validation (ECV) to test the system designed on the Dermoﬁt dataset.
use.other: Publicly available external dataset for performance evaluation.

datasets_used.name: Groningen
datasets_used.description: Digital plain photography images of verified mole and melanoma lesions from Groningen University, Netherlands, publicly available.

use.testing: Used for external cross-validation (ECV) to test the system designed on the Dermoﬁt dataset.
use.other: Publicly available external dataset for performance evaluation.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To determine differences in pictorial properties (texture, structure, shape, color) between moles and melanomas.
Used as a validation method to design the pattern recognition system (PR-system) for discriminating between moles and melanomas.
Used to score the performance of the PR-system on the Dermoﬁt dataset (internal validation) and to test the system on external datasets (Multicenter and Groningen).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper mentions that features were subjected to statistical analysis for determining differences in pictorial properties between moles and melanomas, but it does not report specific hypothesis tests, p-values, or significance levels.

Dermoﬁt.notes: Results obtained using External Cross-Validation (ECV) on the Dermoﬁt images.

Multicenter.notes: Results obtained using External Cross-Validation (ECV) on the Multicenter images.

Groningen.notes: Results obtained using External Cross-Validation (ECV) on the Groningen images.

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Fourteen features evaluating texture (4), structure (5), shape (4), and color (1) were generated from each lesion after outlining the lesion-border and isolating the lesion from the surrounding background.

classification_or_output_head.details: Discrimination between melanocytic nevi and malignant melanomas

preprocessing_techniques: Isolating the lesion from the surrounding background
conclusions: Statistical analysis revealed that melanomas, compared to moles, exhibit lower intensity, less homogenous surfaces, more dark pixels with wider gray-value spectra, more objects of different sizes and gray-levels, more asymmetrical shapes, irregular outlines, abrupt intensity transitions from lesion to background, and more distinct colors.

The adaptable pattern recognition system (PR-system) designed using the Dermoﬁt database achieved an overall accuracy of 94.1%, sensitivity of 82.9%, and specificity of 96.5% on the Dermoﬁt dataset via external cross-validation.
The PR-system maintained good performance on external datasets, achieving 92.0% accuracy on the Multicenter dataset and 76.2% accuracy on the Groningen dataset.

The PR-system designed on the Dermoﬁt image database can be fine-tuned to classify plain photography images of moles and melanomas from other databases that employ different image capturing equipment and protocols with good accuracy.
important_remarks: The study demonstrates significant performance variability across external datasets, with accuracy dropping from 94.1% on the training Dermoﬁt dataset to 76.2% on the Groningen dataset, highlighting sensitivity to differences in image capturing equipment and protocols.

The system was trained on a relatively small dataset (400 images: 330 moles, 70 melanomas) from a single source (Edinburgh University Hospital), which may limit generalizability despite the use of external validation.
The Multicenter dataset used for external validation is small (88 images total), which may result in less robust performance estimates compared to the larger Dermoﬁt and Groningen datasets.
The paper does not report p-values or confidence intervals for the performance metrics, limiting the statistical assessment of the significance of the observed differences between datasets.
The preprocessing step requires manual outlining of the lesion-border, which introduces potential human bias and limits the system's fully automated deployment capability in clinical settings.
